nativeRange.habitat: Origin uncertain; the form has been propagated in European hobby breeding programmes since the 1970s and may represent a captive-developed line of, or a closely related undescribed species to, Rineloricaria lanceolata. Wild Rineloricaria typically inhabit slow- to moderate-flowing tributaries and floodplain margins over sand or leaf-litter substrate.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (preferred over gravel — the species rasps and rests on the bottom)
driftwood pieces and tangled branches for grazing and cover
narrow caves, slate stacks, or short PVC tubes as spawning sites
dried leaf litter (Indian almond, oak, beech) for tannins and biofilm
shaded areas with floating or stem plants to dim the light
gentle to moderate flow with good oxygenation

diet.notes: Readily accepts a wide range of foods despite its loricariid appearance — unlike strictly herbivorous Sturisoma whiptails, this fish takes sinking catfish pellets, algae wafers, frozen bloodworms, daphnia, cyclops, and brine shrimp alongside vegetable matter (blanched courgette, cucumber, kale, spinach). A varied diet that includes both plant and protein components is generally advised; feeding only algae-based foods has been reported to slow growth. Driftwood rasping is normal.

goodWith: other small loricariids of similar temperament
avoidWith: boisterous or aggressive feeders that outcompete it at mealtimes
large cichlids and other territorial bottom fish

summary: The Red Whiptail Catfish is a small, slender red-orange loricariid traded under the L-numbers L010A and L11A. Suspected to be a captive-developed line related to Rineloricaria lanceolata, it has been propagated in European hobby breeding since the 1970s. Peaceful, cave-spawning, and generally well suited to community tanks with gentle flow and driftwood.
careNotes:
The Red Whiptail Catfish is sold under the trade L-numbers L010A (also written L10a) and L011A (L11A), and the names are used essentially interchangeably in the hobby. Both refer to the same red-orange Rineloricaria form, whose precise wild origin and taxonomic identity remain unresolved — it first appeared with European breeders in the 1970s and is widely suspected to be either a captive-selected line of Rineloricaria lanceolata (with which it interbreeds readily) or a closely related undescribed South American species. Either way, all currently traded specimens are captive-bred.

Provide a tank with a fine sand substrate (gravel can damage the delicate ventral surface and barbels) and abundant cover in the form of driftwood, slate caves, short lengths of PVC pipe, and dried botanicals such as Indian almond, oak, or beech leaves. The species tends to be most active in dim, shaded conditions and has been reported to feed and spawn most readily at night; floating plants or background shading help recreate this. Gentle to moderate flow with good surface oxygenation is generally advised, but very strong currents are not required.

The fish tolerates a broader range than many small loricariids, but stable parameters and low nitrate (under ~20 ppm) are important for long-term health and reliable breeding. A cool partial water change has been commonly used as a spawning trigger.

This is one of the more sociable loricariids — small groups (three or more, ideally six in larger tanks) coexist peacefully and behave more naturally than single specimens. Tankmates should be chosen carefully so they do not outcompete the whiptail at feeding time; small characins, Corydoras, dwarf cichlids, and small rasboras are typical good company. Like other scaleless or thinly armoured catfish, copper-based medications are best avoided.

Sex is told apart easily once mature: males develop a covering of short odontodes (bristly spines) on the top of the head and along the leading edge of the pectoral fins; females remain smooth.
breedingNotes:
Breeding is well documented and considered moderate in difficulty — a major reason this form has remained popular for decades. Condition a group of adults on a varied diet and provide several narrow caves or sections of small-bore PVC pipe (around 25–35 mm diameter) on the substrate. Spawning is most commonly triggered by a cool partial water change (a few degrees below the maintenance temperature) combined with fresh, well-oxygenated water.

The female deposits a small clutch — reports range from roughly 45 to about 80 yellow-orange eggs — inside a cave, after which the male takes over sole parental care. He fans and guards the eggs continuously, typically for about 6–8 days until hatching, and may continue defending the cave for several more days while the fry absorb their yolk sacs. Free-swimming fry then leave the cave and can be fed powdered spirulina, micro-pellets, crushed flakes, freshly hatched brine shrimp, and grazed biofilm on driftwood and leaves. Stable, clean water is critical at this stage — fry are noticeably more sensitive to elevated nitrate and to temperature swings than the adults.

primary.name: PlanetCatfish — Rineloricaria sp. (L010A) Cat-eLog
primary.notes: PlanetCatfish is the primary L-number authority for this species but returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch from this environment; husbandry summary cross-built from three independent secondary sources (SeriouslyFish, Fishkeeper.co.uk, AquaInfo) that themselves reference PlanetCatfish data and the published L-number registry. L010A and L011A are the trade designations for the same red-orange Rineloricaria form.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Rineloricaria sp. (L010a/L10a) Red Lizard Catfish
additional.notes: Used as the primary husbandry source after PlanetCatfish 403. Provided: 4.4 in / 11 cm SL adult size, temperature 24–29 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–15 dH, recommended tank ~75x30x30 cm / ~67 L, sand + driftwood + caves setup, omnivorous diet detail, peaceful temperament, cave-spawning breeding biology, male guarding eggs ~4–5 days then fry free-swimming 2–3 days later.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Red Whiptail Catfish (L010A)
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed adult size (12 cm), temperature 24–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.2, hardness up to 15 dH, peaceful temperament, group size 6+, omnivorous diet, ~50 eggs hatching in 6–8 days, fry free-swimming 2–3 days post-hatch.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Rineloricaria sp. L010a Red Lizard Catfish
additional.notes: Third confirming source. Adult size 10–12 cm, temperature 22–28 °C, pH 5.5–7.5, ~45 eggs per clutch with male guarding ~8 days then fry protected ~18 days post-hatch, nocturnal behaviour noted, sand substrate and driftwood preferred. Origin described as uncertain — likely captive-developed colour form of European hobby origin.

additional.name: Quality Marine — Red Lizard Catfish (Rineloricaria sp. L010A)
additional.notes: Trade-side reference. Confirmed first appearance with European breeders in the 1970s, suspected captive-derived origin possibly linked to R. lanceolata, ~10 cm adult size, peaceful and group-tolerant, cave-spawning with male guarding clutch.

fish.breedingNotes: The Red Whiptail Catfish is a cave spawner. The female deposits a clutch of roughly 45 to 80 eggs inside a narrow cave or pipe, after which the male takes over, fanning and guarding the clutch for about 6 to 8 days until hatching and continuing to defend the site for several more days while the fry absorb their yolk sacs. Cool partial water changes are commonly used as a spawning trigger.
